data.description:
Investment Approach

  • Fidelity® International Small Cap Opportunities Fund is a diversified international equity strategy with a small- to mid-cap growth orientation.
  • Our investment approach targets companies with multiyear structural growth prospects, high barriers to entry and attractive valuations based on our earnings forecasts.
  • Investment ideas typically fall into three main categories: structurally attractive growth themes, where investors may be underestimating the durability of growth drivers and long-term earnings power; cyclically out-of-favor companies with limited competition and pricing power, where investors may be focusing on near-term cyclical concerns and discounting long-term prospects; and companies with strong earnings potential whose share prices have fallen due to macroeconomic events.
  • We strive to uncover these companies through in-depth fundamental analysis, working in concert with Fidelity's global research team, with the goal of capturing market upside while limiting downside participation.

Foreign securities are subject to interest rate, currency exchange rate, economic, and political risks. The securities of smaller, less well-known companies can be more volatile than those of larger companies.

Performance Review

For the final quarter of 2025, the fund's Retail Class shares gained 1.53%, trailing the 2.71% advance of the MSCI EAFE Small Cap Index (Net MA). We're disappointed by recent results, but our focus remains on the long term, and in our 17+ years at the helm the portfolio has solidly outperformed its benchmark.

International small-cap stocks extended their upward momentum the past three months, concluding 2025 with both developed and emerging markets notably outpacing U.S. equities. Earnings growth remained positive across most regions, with an acceleration into the end of the year. Several nations have introduced policies to support domestic growth, from favorable fiscal initiatives in Europe to policy measures aimed at bolstering consumption and the broader equity market in China. Against this backdrop, our active, "bottom-up" approach to investing – applying fundamental research to uncover companies with compelling characteristics – lagged modestly this quarter. By country, security selection in Japan and Germany notably detracted most from the fund's relative result. Out-of-benchmark exposure to Canada and Taiwan also hurt. Sector-wise, positioning in materials, communication services and information technology were performance headwinds, as was avoiding utilities entirely.

On a stock-specific basis, Baltic Classifieds (-34%) – an online classified ads portal provider serving Lithuania, Estonia and Latvia – was the largest relative detractor. Shares of the firm fell sharply on December 4 after management reported mixed financial results amid a marked slowdown in its core auto business, in addition to issuing a warning about future profit-margin compression. Consequently, we pared the position considerably this period. Elsewhere, out-of-index stakes in Canada-based intermediate precious metal royalty company OR Royalties (-12%) and Lumine Group (-33%), a Canadian private equity firm focusing on software, telecom and media businesses, further weighed on performance.

Conversely, picks in France, Italy, Sweden, the Netherlands and the U.K. meaningfully contributed. From a sector perspective, we added the most value within consumer discretionary, led by durables & apparel stocks. An outsized position in Britain's Games Workshop Group (+32%), a manufacturer of miniature tabletop wargames, led the way among individual holdings. In November, management reported a 15% rise in semiannual revenue and raised its profit outlook. An overweight in Interpump Group (+20%) – an Italian industrial manufacturer specializing in high-pressure piston pumps and hydraulic products – and a non-index position in British telecom investment company Zegona Communications (ZEGLF) (+28%) also were noteworthy relative contributors.

Outlook and Positioning

As the year came to a close, the global economy remained in a solid, though uneven, expansion amid policy crosscurrents. Despite ongoing risks to the global cycle, we also see potential tailwinds. For example, the share of countries reporting improved manufacturing conditions rose, suggesting global industrial activity may be improving after a three-year soft patch. From a valuation standpoint, cyclically adjusted price-to-earnings ratios for international equities appear relatively attractive compared with the U.S. Moreover, when considering investors' expectations for earnings growth over the next 12 months, the forward P/E ratios for stocks abroad are substantially lower than their U.S. counterparts.

In addition, despite the U.S. dollar's roughly 10% decline in 2025, our analysis suggests international currencies remain undervalued versus the greenback, which historically has been favorable for developed- and emerging-markets returns.

The fund's geographic positioning remained fairly consistent the past three months. Swedish, British and Dutch equities were the most sizable overweights. We also maintained meaningful non-benchmark exposure to Canada, the U.S. and Taiwan. On the other hand, Australia, Japan and Switzerland accounted for the largest relative underweights. In terms of any material changes, the portfolio's allocation to Sweden ticked up a bit versus the prior quarter, whereas positioning in Germany modestly decreased in Q4.

Sector-wise, industrials overtook technology this quarter to become the biggest overweight as of year-end, led by a sizable stake in capital goods firms. Tech and consumer discretionary were the only other areas of emphasis versus the benchmark. Within the former, holdings in the hardware & equipment, as well as software & services categories, were noteworthy. Among consumer discretionary stocks, consumer services was a notable industry overweight. Our exposure to consumer-oriented segments of the market and financials rose somewhat this quarter, offset by a noticeable decline in communication services. In contrast, the largest sector underweights were real estate, materials and financials.

Top active equity positions were Swedish technology solutions provider AddTech, as well as Interpump and Games Workshop (both previously mentioned). Conversely, we continued to avoid several benchmark components that failed to meet our investment criteria, including Tower Semiconductor (TSEM), BAWAG Group (BWAGF) and Weir Group (WEIGF).

R-Squared measures how a fund's performance correlates with a benchmark index's performance and shows what portion of it can be explained by the performance of the overall market/index. R-Squared ranges from 0, meaning no correlation, to 1, meaning perfect correlation. An R-Squared value of less than 0.5 indicates that annualized alpha and beta are not reliable performance statistics.

Sharpe Ratio is a measure of historical risk-adjusted performance. It is calculated by dividing the fund's excess returns (the fund's average annual return for the period minus the 3-month "risk free" return rate) and dividing it by the standard deviation of the fund's returns. The higher the ratio, the better the fund's return per unit of risk. The three month "risk free" rate used is the 90-day Treasury Bill rate.

Standard Deviation is a statistical measurement of the dispersion of a fund's return over a specified time period. Standard deviation does not indicate how the fund actually performed, but merely indicates the volatility of its returns over time.

Tracking Error is the divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark, creating an unexpected profit or loss.

data.title: Ralph Lauren: Coveted Brand With Strong Outlook, Shares Fairly Valued
data.description:
Ralph Lauren Corporation (RL) is enjoying a strong run of better-than-expected operating performance and outstanding share price performance.

Though shares in the luxury lifestyle product company are down on a YTD basis, RL is still sitting on gains of over 40% in the past year. The stock is also continuing to trade near the upper boundaries of its 52-week range despite the broader market volatility.

The outperformance in an era of more cautious consumer spending in the face of higher inflation could perhaps come as a surprise to some. The company, however, has made significant inroads with the younger generations, and this has served as one significant tailwind.

At current trading levels, I’m neutral on the stock but do believe shares are worth a watch for any meaningful pullbacks that may occur as a result of continuing market volatility.

RL Stock Key Metrics

Despite its significant rise over the last five years, shares in RL still trade at what I would view as a reasonable valuation.

At 20x forward earnings, shares aren’t necessarily richly priced, especially when compared to the multiples commanded elsewhere in the broader markets. Multiples are also just a touch higher than RL’s own historical averages.

The Seeking Alpha (“SA”) quants don’t exactly see it that way, with the quants grading RL as an “F” on valuation. This is likely due to RL’s premium pricing relative to the sector averages. In my view, however, RL’s operating performance and growth metrics justify the premium.

Others across both the SA community and Wall Street would likely agree, with both analyst populations largely seeing shares as a Buy at current trading levels.

Wall Street, in fact, sees nearly 20% upside ahead, with shares seen as fairly valued at the $405/share mark. This is somewhat ambitious in today’s market, in my opinion, but I believe the long-term trajectory is there.

RL Stock Operating Performance

RL’s last reported quarter was its fiscal Q3, released in early February, and in my view, the results then were impressive across the board.

Importantly, the strength wasn’t isolated to just one part of the business. Revenue grew 10% in the quarter, ahead of expectations, with equal contributions across geographies, channels, and product categories.

From a geographic footprint, Asia once again stood out, with over 20% growth, while North America and Europe also posted solid gains despite tougher comparisons.

What stood out most to me, however, was the quality of the growth. The company continues to lean into its premium positioning, with full-price sell-throughs coming in stronger than expected during the critical holiday season.

This allowed RL to pull back on promotions, driving an 18% increase in average unit retail and contributing to a 140 basis point expansion in gross margin. That’s a strong signal to me that brand heat and pricing power remain intact.

From a profitability standpoint, operating income grew 21%, with operating margins expanded to over 20%. This came even as the company continued to invest in marketing and digital capabilities.

What Is The Outlook For RL Stock?

I view RL’s forward outlook as positive due in part to both continued operating momentum and a brand that is clearly resonating with a new generation of consumers.

From a guidance standpoint, CFO Justin Picicci provided a raised full-year revenue outlook in his Q3 commentary. This was high single-digit to low double-digit growth, up from prior expectations, alongside projected operating margin expansion of 100 to 140 basis points.

Geographically, Asia is expected to remain a key growth driver with mid-teens growth, while North America and Europe are both projected to deliver steady mid-single-digit gains.

Importantly, the company is expected to continue prioritizing full-price selling and a more disciplined promotional environment. This should further support margin expansion over time despite near-term tariff pressures.

One of the more significant elements of the story, in my view, is the brand’s growing traction with younger consumers.

What was once seen as a legacy, “older generation” brand has found new life with Gen Z, fueled by social media trends, digital engagement, and a broader shift toward “quiet luxury” and nostalgia-driven fashion.

As can be seen, this is translating into strong customer acquisition, with millions of new, younger consumers entering the ecosystem.

Is RL Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

I view RL as a solid investment with a positive outlook. However, I believe much of the near-term upside already appears reflected in the stock.

For one, RL is continuing to put out consistent revenue growth, is expanding margins, and, perhaps most importantly, is elevating the overall quality of its business through full-price selling and disciplined brand management.

I also believe that RL is successfully repositioning itself for the next decade, particularly through its growing relevance with younger consumers. That’s not an easy feat for a legacy brand, and it speaks to the effectiveness of management’s strategy and the enduring strength of the brand itself, in my view.

That said, shares trading near the upper end of their yearly range and at a modest premium to historical multiples leave less room for multiple expansion in the near term.

While Wall Street may see additional upside, I think expectations are already fairly well baked in, especially given the still uncertain consumer environment.

All considered, I view shares as a Hold. This is a name I’d be comfortable owning for the long term, but I’d prefer to be more opportunistic and look to add on any meaningful pullbacks rather than chasing at current levels.

data.title: Persimmon: Attractively Priced, With Leverage To Potential Building Boom
data.description:
Introduction

Share prices of listed UK housebuilders have reacted very negatively to the fresh round of economic uncertainty triggered by the Iran conflict. As shown in Exhibit 1, Vistry Group PLC (BVHMF), Taylor Wimpey plc (TWODF), Persimmon Plc (PSMMF), and BARRATT REDROW PLC (BTDPF) have all materially underperformed the FTSE 100 since late February 2026. Vistry has been hit the hardest, but this in part reflects certain stock-specific issues. For medium-term-focused investors, all four of these stocks are now probably in good value-buying territory.

Exhibit 1:

While Vistry and Barratt Redrow offer the greatest potential upside, they are also higher-risk propositions. My preferred pick of the sector is Persimmon PLC, which I see as a higher-quality, lower-risk play in its sector. In this note I’ll explain why Persimmon, which trades on the London Stock Exchange under the code PSN, looks well-placed to benefit from medium-term structural tailwinds for the UK housebuilders and why value-focused investors should be willing to embrace the opportunity that the current price weakness has provided.

Company Overview

Persimmon was founded in 1972 by Duncan Davidson, who established a small regional builder based out of York. After successfully expanding its operations beyond Yorkshire, the company completed its first acquisition in 1984 and listed on the London Stock Exchange a year later. Further large acquisitions were completed in 1996 (Ideal Homes), 2001 (Beazer Homes), and 2006 (Westbury Homes), helping to give the builder a national presence. Today, Persimmon is one of the UK’s largest housebuilders, operating through 29 regional offices and still headquartered in York.

With the bulk of the business targeting budget-friendly, volume builds, PSN has wisely invested heavily in its own supply chain. By manufacturing its own bricks (through Brickworks), roof tiles (through Tileworks), and timber frames (through Space4), PSN has reduced its reliance on external suppliers and established a competitive advantage in terms of build efficiency and the ability to manage building cost inflation.

PSN is best known for its focus on the affordable and first-time buyer segments, but the group also builds higher-end homes through its Charles Church brand. Persimmon Homes is the group’s core brand; it builds a range of traditional family housing and makes up around 75% of group revenue and gross profit.

The Charles Church brand differentiates itself from Persimmon Homes by delivering larger, higher-specification housing in carefully selected premium locations. Charles Church currently contributes around 14% of group revenue, with its higher margin sales generating a slightly larger contribution to total group gross profit.

PSN uses the Westbury Partnerships brand to participate in the UK’s affordable social and build-to-rent (‘BTR’) housing sector. Partnerships sells its homes to housing associations and institutional investors. Given its focus on the affordable end of the UK property market and its more wholesale model, it is not surprising that Partnerships is PSN’s lowest gross margin brand segment. Partnerships currently contribute about 11% of group revenue and 9% of total group gross profit.

There Are Good Reasons For The Market To Be Spooked

Late last week, the Iran war pushed the yield on UK 10-year government bonds up through the 5% barrier, to levels not seen since the global financial crisis. With a heavy reliance on imported gas, an already struggling UK economy has been seen as particularly vulnerable to rising energy costs.

Shorter-dated UK bonds have been hit even harder, reflecting expectations of Bank of England interest rate hikes. The two-year UK government bond yield is currently around 4.4% and had risen by around 100 bp when it touched 4.6% late last week.

Higher bond yields are feeding through into higher two-year and five-year fixed-term mortgage rates. These fixed-rate mortgage products dominate the mortgage lending market in the UK. Higher mortgage rates will dampen buyer demand for new homes and also limit the amount of debt that banks can prudently extend to borrowers. With potential buyers of PSN’s new homes also now feeling that they are about to go through another period of cost-of-living inflation stress, the near-term outlook for new home sales has undoubtedly taken a sharp downward turn relative to the pre-Iran war position.

In addition to the negative impact on demand for new housing, the Iran war increases the potential for further increases to building costs, both in terms of materials and labor. It should be noted that PSN is less exposed to this gross margin downside risk thanks to its strategy of in-housing large parts of its supply chain (bricks, tiles, timber frames).

The Bigger Picture Remains Supportive – 1.5 Million New Homes for England

When participating in markets, it is important to stay on top of issues that affect the near-term outlook for economies and industries. But it is often the case that major unexpected events, what we might think of as "shocks," can cloud the more significant drivers of value that are expected to play out over timeframes of months and years rather than weeks; I currently believe this to be the case with UK housebuilders and the market’s reaction to the Iran war. So let’s step back and consider the bigger picture.

In December 2024, following a convincing election victory in the preceding summer, the UK’s Labour government released a paper called "Plan For Change - Milestones for Mission-Led Government." A major component of Labour’s plan for "national renewal" was to address the UK’s housing crisis through the delivery of 1.5 million net new homes in England over a five-year period. In support of this goal, the government announced its intention to overhaul the UK’s planning system, targeting major reforms to planning rules and the fast-tracking of 150 planning decisions on major infrastructure projects.

As shown in Exhibit 2 below, after flatlining at slightly below 150,000 per annum net new dwellings in England in the post-global financial crisis era, new builds had increased to around 250,000 per annum prior to the Covid pandemic. Labour’s target of ~300,000 per annum net new dwellings for England is therefore ambitious relative to the pre-Covid run-rate, and even more so when measured against the post-pandemic activity level of ~225,000 per annum. If Labour’s plan to stimulate new housebuilding works, volume housebuilders such as PSN have the opportunity to very significantly increase their annual sales volumes.

Exhibit 2:

A recent report released by the Ministry of Housing, Communities & Local Government on March 19 confirms that progress to date on Labour’s plan has been disappointing. The report states that England’s net new dwellings for the 2024/2025 financial year were ~209,000 and that the estimated net new dwellings for the period April 01, 2025, to March 15, 2026, were ~191,000. Extrapolating the 2025 run rate, we get to expected net new dwellings for the 2025/2026 financial year of around 200,000.

With net new dwellings running at ~100,000 per annum less than Labour’s target, it is clear that the government needs to push things along more aggressively. In November 2025, the government announced its Social and Affordable Homes Programme 2026 to 2036 (‘SAHP’), which will provide grant funding of at least £27.3bn to support affordable housing development in England (outside London). SAHP includes a ‘Strategic Partnership’ funding route targeted at large housebuilders, applications for which close in mid-April 2026.

The UK government released yet another update about its housing plan on March 23. The locations of seven proposed new towns have been published - each location is expected to deliver at least 10,000 homes, with several targeting 40,000 or more over a build period that extends into decades.

Whilst the government is making progress on the policy and budgetary front, comments from PSN and peers point to little having actually changed as yet at ground level. If Labour is going to get anywhere near to its 1.5m net new dwellings target, activity levels need to ramp up massively, and soon. Empowered by modifications to the National Planning Policy Framework, council authorities in England will be under great pressure to deliver on mandatory local housing targets that the Labour government reinstated in late 2024.

I think that the dark mood that has fallen over the UK housebuilding sector since the Iran war began is likely to trigger some sort of government response to stimulate the market. On the supply side, throwing further resources at local planning departments is a lower-cost option but is less likely to quickly ramp up activity, in my view. Releasing underutilized public land for new housing development is another useful supply-side lever, but this is also unlikely to generate rapid change.

Demand-side stimulus such as stamp-duty reform or a new ‘help-to-buy’ scheme could be on the agenda; such initiatives would be enthusiastically welcomed by PSN and peers. PSN’s Persimmon Homes brand targets lower-cost housing and would likely be a significant winner in a scenario in which the government directs stimulus at the affordable end of the housing market. A demand stimulus restricted to first-time buyers would also play to PSN’s strength; close to a third of PSN’s private sector completions in FY25 were to first-time buyers. I would note, however, that fiscal budgetary constraints may limit what, if anything, can be done by Labour on the demand-side front.

FY25 Result Highlights

PSN released FY25 results on March 10, 2026. The market responded positively to the update, pushing PSN up by ~12% in early trading and closing the day with a gain of some 4.5%. PSN’s FY25 numbers were mostly solid, and in my view warranted being well-received by the market. In the bullet points below, I’ll briefly highlight some key positives and negatives that caught my eye while reviewing the FY25 materials.

Key FY25 positives:

  • New home completions were up +12% on FY24, with all three brands (Persimmon Homes, Charles Church, and Partnerships) reporting growth.
  • Outlets (or active sales locations) increased by 3% to 277. This was against a backdrop of a 2% decline in outlets at the industry level. PSN says that the plan to reach 300 outlets within the next couple of years is on track.
  • With average selling prices increasing by +4%, new housing revenue growth was impressive at +16%.
  • Profit growth was strong; gross profit was up by +13% relative to FY24, and operating profit growth came in at +17%.
  • The operating margin improved to 14.3%, up from 14.1% reported in FY24. PSN is doing a good job on expense control, with FY25 operating expenses as a percentage of revenue coming in at 6.2%, down from 6.4% in FY24. In the FY25 Q&A session, CFO Andrew Duxbury confirmed that PSN is expecting further operating margin gains in FY26E, at a similar level to the improvement that came through in FY25.
  • Build quality metrics improved further in FY25 and are now some 31% higher than in FY21. Build quality has been problematic for Persimmon in the past, so it is important for the group to maintain its current high standards. The group achieved its fourth consecutive year of 5-star homebuilder status with the Home Builders Federation.

Key FY25 negatives:

  • The group gross profit margin declined to 19.8%, down from 20.3% in FY24. The gross margin decline was driven by product mix, with revenue growth in the lower-margin Partnerships segment exceptionally strong at +36%, well above that of Persimmon Homes (+12%) and Charles Church (+21%).
  • FY25’s return on capital employed (ROCE) of 11.7% is a long way behind the PSN medium-term target of 20%.
  • Net cash has fallen from £259m a year earlier to £117m at FY25. Land creditors have also increased significantly, coming in at -£623m as compared against -£423m at FY24. The reduction in net cash has been driven mainly by a net investment in land. Looking at adjusted gearing as the combination of net cash/debt and land creditors, PSN has flagged that this metric will increase in FY26E.

Outlook Considerations

PSN’s FY25 result management commentary pointed to a very good start to FY26E, as highlighted by the extract from PSN’s earnings release below:

In the first nine weeks of this year our net private sales rate per outlet per week was 0.73, up 9% compared to the same period last year (2025: 0.67). The private average selling price in the order book is up 6%, which combined with increased reservations has resulted in a 9% increase in our private forward sales position to £1.25bn as at 1 March compared with a year ago (2025: £1.15bn). Total forward sales as at 1 March have increased by 6% to £1.80bn (2025: £1.69bn).

Source: PSN FY25 Earnings Release, page 8.

On the assumption that the Iran conflict would have little impact on PSN’s outlook, the company flagged expected completions for FY26E of between 12,000 and 12,500, representing volume growth of around 2.9% at the midpoint. PSN guided (again on the assumption of no material impact from the Iran war) to underlying operating profit towards the upper end of the consensus range (being £486m to £517m). Underlying profit before tax was guided to come in at ~£470m, in line with the consensus mean.

To put the FY26E guidance in context, Exhibit 3 sets out a summary of PSN’s operating and financial performance over the last five years. It shows that FY26E guidance reflected solid but not spectacular growth relative to FY25 and a level of profitability a long way below both the group’s medium-term 20% return on capital target and the very high returns achieved during the pandemic recovery period.

Exhibit 3:

At the time of writing, it is almost two weeks since PSN released its FY25 results, and the Iran conflict remains far from settled. As such, it seems likely that PSN’s FY26E result will materially undershoot the FY26E guidance recently issued.

The extent of lost FY26E profitability is pretty much impossible to predict at present. However, I am more focused on the medium-to-long-term outlook for PSN. I have already touched on the potential for PSN to achieve strong volume growth due to Labour’s housing plans, but I think it is also worth highlighting the group’s margin improvement story, which I believe can play out even without a UK housebuilding boom.

This margin improvement story relies on the fading of previous building cost inflation impacts over time, and as such it will be something of a slow burn. High building cost inflation from late 2022 to early 2024 is still embedded in PSN’s inventory. This is nicely highlighted in Exhibit 4 below.

Exhibit 4:

It normally takes four to five years for PSN to move from land acquisition to property sale completion, and so the margin drag associated with post-pandemic cost inflation will not be fully off the books until 2028/2029. PSN says that ~75% of expected FY26E property completions will be ‘encumbered’ with this past high building cost inflation impact, but that FY27E will have less of a margin headwind. Assuming that the Iran conflict does not trigger a fresh round of high building cost inflation, PSN looks set to benefit from healthy margin expansion over the final few years of the decade.

Key Downside Risk - Building Safety Remediation Provisions

The Building Safety Act (‘BSA’) came into force in April 2022, in response to the Grenfell Tower tragedy, which claimed the lives of 72 people. The purpose of the legislation is to make homes safer by giving residents and owners more rights, powers, and protections in regard to correcting safety defects in historic buildings. The BSA, implemented through the Building Safety Programme, created wide-reaching implications across the UK building industry, impacting large companies working on multi-million-pound construction projects, through to small businesses trading in repair, maintenance, and improvement work.

PSN has been proactive in its response to the BSA. In 2022, the group signed the Building Safety Pledge (England) and worked with the government to agree on a 'Long-Form Contract' that turned the pledge into a legal agreement. In December 2025, PSN was the first housebuilder to sign the Scottish government’s developer remediation contract.

At an industry level, the cladding and fire safety remediation issue is far from resolved, with work expected to continue for several more years. As with other listed UK housebuilders, PSN holds large remediation provisions in relation to the BSA. The provision of £226m as at FY25 is based on management’s best estimates of the costs of completing works to ensure fire safety on affected buildings under PSN’s ownership and to work with and support owners and other relevant stakeholders on buildings it has developed.

PSN forecasts that ~£83m of the provision will be utilized during FY26E. The calculation of the provision requires multiple judgments and estimates to be made in respect of the cost of remediation works. This is a highly complex process, and there is significant scope for actual costs to vary materially from PSN’s estimates. Exhibit 5 shows PSN’s current position regarding BSA remediation, along with progress made over the last two years.

Exhibit 5:

PSN says that ~90% of known affected developments have been fully tendered, have active ongoing site work, or have been completed. The bulk of PSN’s remediation spend is expected to occur over the next two years. In the FY25 management speech, CFO Andrew Duxbury said that PSN expects to be on site at all affected developments by the end of the year and that PSN continues to track ahead of the overall industry position in regard to BSA remediation.

In order to ensure that my investment case for PSN is prudently set, and recognizing the high degree of uncertainty sitting behind PSN’s balance sheet provision, for valuation purposes I have allowed for a 30% top-up ‘safety margin’ for remediation provisions. This 30% safety margin assumption is not particularly scientific, but it is based on my past experience of analyzing similar legacy remediation provision programs for listed companies across a range of sectors. I would not regard a 50% safety margin as being ridiculously conservative, and at the other end, I would say that a 10% safety margin would factor in too little valuation downside protection.

Valuation Assessment

Exhibit 6 summarizes the adjusted enterprise value (AEV) to EBIT framework that I have used to assess PSN’s valuation appeal. To calculate AEV, I have allowed for -£623m owed to land creditors, the BSA provision of -£226m, and a safety buffer on top of this provision of -£68m (being the 30% allowance referenced above).

For the EBIT scenarios, I have made use of the FY26E consensus range that PSN referenced in its FY25 materials. The calculations provide AEV/EBIT multiples based on the lower and upper end of consensus, along with the midpoint of the consensus range. In addition, I have considered a bear case EBIT scenario that uses EBIT 10% below the bottom of the FY26E consensus range.

Exhibit 6:

At £11.425 per share (PSN London Stock Exchange closing price March 23), my calculations point to an AEV/EBIT multiple of 8.45x, using the FY26E consensus range EBIT mid-point. This multiple strikes me as very low for a business that has considerable potential to grow its earnings via volume growth and margin expansion, with the latter not dependent on the former. The bear case AEV/EBIT multiple of 10.2x is not particularly high, and this gives me confidence that downside risk is limited.

Closing Remarks & Rating

Whilst I have painted a mostly bullish picture above, I think it is important for readers to understand that the UK housebuilding sector is highly cyclical. If we were to enter a deep and lasting economic downturn, I suspect that PSN and peers would underperform the broader UK equity market. Were the UK’s state finances in a healthier position, I would be inclined to suggest that cyclical downturn risk would be mitigated by government stimulus of the housing sector – but that is not the reality of the present situation.

The above health warning having been made, my view is that the impact of the Iran conflict on PSN will prove to be relatively limited and certainly nowhere near significant enough to offset the upside potential associated with Labour’s housing plans and the likely expansion in PSN’s operating margin, which is expected to ramp up from FY27E onwards. Further, in the case that we do see a fresh round of building cost inflation triggered by the Iran conflict, I see PSN as a relative winner in its sector due to its strategic decision to in-house supply of key building materials.

PSN is currently trading at around 15% below the level it reached when the market first digested its FY25 update. Perhaps that initial market reaction to the result was overly positive given the uncertainties around the Iran conflict, but I think that we can expect much of that 15% loss to rapidly reverse if things settle down in the Iran region in the near future.

Taking a longer-term perspective, I see PSN as the least risky way to obtain exposure to what has the potential to be a multi-year period of high-volume output for UK housebuilders. At the current share price of ~£11.40 per share, I think the market’s attention is overly focused on near-term downside risks and that the bigger value picture is being missed. I conclude this review with a Buy rating, noting that, as usual, my assessment of PSN is based on a medium-term outlook.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Protalix Revenue Miss Overshadowed By Milestone-Backed 2026 Guidance
data.description:
Thesis

Protalix BioTherapeutics, Inc. (PLX) for FY25 reported a lot weaker-than-expected results. GAAP EPS was a loss of $0.08, missing the consensus by about $0.07, with revenue reaching $52.7 million. Revenue was down just 1% year over year but missed expectations by about $5.2 million. So there were much softer sales, but also some hefty pipeline investments. Now, despite this miss, the company still issued a pretty strong FY26 guidance of $78 to $83 million, well above consensus, which was at $71.8 million. I think we can put this down to an Elfabrio milestone and some strong expected commercial growth on the back of the European approval.

My upside would then come from faster-than-expected Elfabrio adoption, with the new driver being the E4W dosing advantage, as I'll explain. This should positively impact Chiesi's revenues and improve the overall margin. I also see some additional upside potentially coming from a U.S. dosing approval or strong PRX-115 data.

FY25 Earnings Overview

Back in 2024, we did see some short-term profitability from Protalix; however, 2025 is more reflective of investment-driven losses. Total revenue came in at $52.7 million, only slightly down from $53.4 million in FY24, and primarily due to a decline in sales to its partner Chiesi. The good news was that the drop was partially offset by a much stronger demand from Pfizer for Elelyso supply needs. We also saw stable contributions from Brazil’s Fiocruz. As for the overall revenue mix, it’s still very much dependent on product sales rather than high-margin licensing income, the latter of which remained rather minimal. What also didn't make for great viewing was the fact that the cost of goods sold rose about 11%, so again, this would indicate margin pressure, mostly from production scale and partner mix. So if we take that combination of flat revenue and rising costs, it compressed operating performance. As a result, the company posted an operating loss of $5.5 million, compared to a $3.9 million operating profit the year before.

If we take a look at the spending side, the big driver behind the bottom-line deterioration was simply a sharp increase in R&D spending. This jumped about 51% to $19.6 million, with Protalix now pivoting toward pipeline expansion. On that note, the Phase 2 PRX-115 gout program would be taking up much of that R&D spend. I see this investment here as a deliberate trade-off in the sense that near-term earnings will report substantially lower to build more long-term value in those higher-potential therapeutic areas.

To see the R&D spending impact, there was some degree of cost control in SG&A, which dropped about 4%, but I realistically see the R&D surge, along with some minor financial expenses and tax impacts, resulting in a net loss of $6.6 million, versus net income of $2.9 million back in 2024. On the balance sheet, things still look positive. The company managed to end FY25 with $30.3 million in cash and no outstanding debt. Management is also projecting a much stronger $50 million cash position in 2026 since they have a $25 million milestone payment tied to Elfabrio’s EU approval. So even though we did see weakening profitability metrics, we have to put it in context with the strategic reinvestment phase that should hopefully be supported by partnerships and upcoming catalysts. It does leave some upside for potential revenue acceleration in 2026.

New Dosing Advantage

So, much of my previous thesis rested on the EU approval of the once-every-4-weeks (E4W) dosing regimen for Elfabrio. I viewed this as a genuinely meaningful commercial/clinical inflection point, which I saw as having a high chance of approval. Now, it’s worth noting that post-approval, we shouldn’t just view this as a regulatory formality but rather a real advantage for Elfabrio. You see, in rare diseases like Fabry, the overall treatment burden can be a pretty major barrier to adherence. Patients usually require lifelong intravenous infusions every two weeks. These are disruptive and physically taxing simply because of the frequent hospital visits and IV administration. So a big burden here would be the logistical strain.

So by extending dosing to once every four weeks without compromising the efficacy, Elfabrio can now basically cut that strain in half. For example, a patient previously needing 26 infusions per year would now require only about 13, which means fewer hospital visits, less time off work, and just overall improved quality of life. This is the kind of convenience advantage that we can expect Protalix to push through sales. And what we want to now see as a result is new patient starts and switches from competing therapies, especially when physicians have to weigh long-term adherence and patient preference. As for the competitive landscape, the current competitors do not offer a comparable regimen, so it should create a clear differentiation lever for Chiesi’s commercial teams. I see this as being particularly true in Europe, where we usually see treatment decisions get influenced by quality-of-life improvements as much as clinical endpoints.

Now, we need to place that advantage alongside Protalix’s 2026 growth outlook, which I see aligning. Management is guiding for about $78 to $83 million in total revenue, with the step-change mainly driven by Elfabrio’s expansion. We can also expect a shift in revenue mix toward Chiesi going forward. Keep in mind that Chiesi-related revenues tend to be higher margin and more scalable because they are tied more to a growing patient base rather than episodic orders like Elelyso, which we’ve seen fluctuate due to partner inventory needs, as was the case with Pfizer in 2025. So my updated thesis would then include Elfabrio adoption increasing, helped, of course, by the new dosing flexibility.

As per management, Chiesi’s contribution is expected to grow to $33 to $35 million in 2026, so it would then make up over 50% growth and a much larger share of total revenue. I think this new mix setup should improve the predictability of earnings over time, even if we do see quarterly results remain a bit uneven due to ordering patterns. So in that sense, the dosing approval doesn’t just boost the near-term sales outlook here, but it helps with some credibility around that guidance.

One Area Of Concern

Just to mention briefly, one area I see some concern or rather unpredictability is that while the EU approval of the E4W dosing regimen is a strong sign, there is still no clear timeline for a similar approval in the U.S. So that would then leave a key upside catalyst unresolved. That being said, the regulatory momentum in Europe that we just saw, especially with supportive clinical and real-world data, can potentially help de-risk and then accelerate the FDA pathway. But I think because of this uncertainty, the market appears to be only partially pricing in a U.S. label expansion currently, which means a future approval could represent some incremental upside via improved competitiveness/adoption in the largest market.

Valuation

If you glance at the valuation page for Protalix, things appear particularly cheap. They’re a small-cap biotech that’s being priced more on forward potential rather than current earnings stability. However, quite a bit is being discounted here. The stock trades at roughly 1.9x forward EV to sales and 2.2x forward price to sales, which would be a 38% to 40% discount to the healthcare sector. So the market is assigning a conservative value to its commercial base in Elfabrio and Elelyso, despite expected 2026 revenues of $78 to $83 million, not to mention the expected $25 million milestone boost. Elsewhere, the forward EV to EBIT sits at 8.6x, also about a 39% discount to peers. So the expectation would be a return to profitability, but with a limited premium assigned to that recovery.

At the same time, we have to focus on the clear skepticism, particularly around the durability and growth trajectory of the business. Now, the forward price-to-earnings of 9.9x appears cheap versus the sector, but this is based on a temporary earnings rebound, with estimates implying a sharp deterioration there afterwards. 2027 EPS is expected to decline pretty significantly. I see this as the market discounting several risks, including reliance on their current partners like Pfizer/Chiesi. There is also some volatility in product demand, along with the uncertainty of its pipeline, particularly with regard to PRX-115, which is still in Phase 2.

Looking Ahead

So the good things we just saw were that Elfabrio revenue is picking up. It's also highly 4Q25 weighted, which comes down a bit to revenue recognition in the sense that we usually see Chiesi place orders towards the end of the year. So it could be the case that the earlier quarters look a bit weaker even if the underlying demand is still strong, which is the main thing here. Expectations are for growth of about 50%, driven by that European geographic expansion and improved U.S. coverage, which should show up in a large Q4 catch-up going forward. So if this plays out, there should be more upside for Protalix. Going forward, investors should focus a bit on the ordering patterns and uptake. We want to see sustained demand growth, as well as continued progress in the PRX-115 pipeline.

data.title: John Hancock Multimanager Lifestyle Balanced Portfolio Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly commentary

  • Most asset categories produced positive returns in the final three months of 2025, reflecting the favorable backdrop for the world financial markets.
  • The fund posted a gain but slightly underperformed its benchmark.
  • Asset allocation contributed to performance, while underlying manager results detracted.

Market review and outlook

Global equities registered solid gains in the fourth quarter, helping the major, broad-based indexes record their third consecutive year of double-digit returns. Performance was uneven over the first half of the quarter due to concerns that AI-related stocks were in a bubble, but the market staged an impressive rebound and went on to achieve new all-time highs by year end. A continued decline in inflation enabled the U.S. Federal Reserve to enact two quarter-point interest rate cuts, boosting sentiment. In addition, corporate earnings were robust and world economic growth remained positive. Emerging- and developed-market international equities outperformed the United States, continuing a trend that was in place for the full year. Within the U.S. market, the value style outpaced growth as investors rotated toward opportunities outside of AI-related stocks.

Global bonds logged only slightly positive total returns amid a growing consensus that most central banks were largely finished easing policy. Credit-oriented market segments continued to outperform, primarily as a result of their yield advantage.

Contributors and detractors

The majority of the benefit from allocation came from the fund's overweight in developed-market international equities and corresponding underweight in the United States. We have favored the non-U.S. markets for some time based on their attractive relative valuations, and this aspect of our positioning paid off in both the quarter and the year. The fund's real assets portfolio also contributed to relative performance, primarily as a result of a position in metals and mining stocks. The sector performed very well in the quarter thanks to impressive rallies in both precious and industrial metals. An overweight in equities versus bonds was a further plus. On the other hand, an overweight in U.S. mid caps detracted. The fund's fixed-income positioning had a neutral effect on results, with the benefit of holding emerging-market bonds offset by weakness in the portfolio's modest holding in U.S. Treasury STRIPS.

No single area stood out as a key driver of underperformance for the fund's active managers; instead, the shortfall was primarily a result of very small differences for a wide range of individual holdings.

Portfolio changes

We retained diversified exposure across the full spectrum of the world financial markets, while also making some modest changes on the margin to capitalize on categories where we saw attractive valuations and higher long-term return potential. We remain confident in the fund's positioning as we move into 2026, based on our belief that diversification is likely to become increasingly important. For most of the past year, investors would have been rewarded by simply holding the narrow group of mega-cap U.S. technology-related stocks that continued to dominate performance. However, we began to see this backdrop evolve somewhat over the final three months of the year with a notable broadening of leadership. While it's impossible to say if this is a sign of a larger shift, we believe investors are better served by maintaining broad-based diversification rather than relying on another year of positive relative performance for a handful of companies. We think the fund is well positioned for these circumstances through its ability to seek opportunities across a wide range of asset classes.

This commentary reflects the views of the portfolio managers named and is subject to change as market and other conditions warrant. No forecasts are guaranteed. What you should know before investing

The portfolio's performance depends on the advisor's skill in determining asset class allocations, the mix of underlying funds, and the performance of those underlying funds. The portfolio is subject to the same risks as the underlying funds and exchange-traded funds in which it invests: Stocks and bonds can decline due to adverse issuer, market, regulatory, or economic developments; foreign investing, especially in emerging markets, has additional risks, such as currency and market volatility and political and social instability; the securities of small companies are subject to higher volatility than those of larger, more established companies; and high-yield bonds are subject to additional risks, such as increased risk of default. Fixed-income investments are subject to interest-rate and credit risk; their value will normally decline as interest rates rise or if an issuer is unable or unwilling to make principal or interest payments. Liquidity—the extent to which a security may be sold or a derivative position closed without negatively affecting its market value, if at all—may be impaired by reduced trading volume, heightened volatility, rising interest rates, and other market conditions. Please see the portfolio's prospectus for additional risks.

data.title: STMicroelectronics: DC And Robotics Opportunities Re-Ignited Enthusiasm (Upgrade)
data.description:
The near-term outlook for key semiconductor customers in the auto and industrial markets is still murky, but exposure to growth opportunities in data centers, sensing, robotics, and power continues to resonate well with investors. To that end, STMicroelectronics’ (STM) recent efforts to highlight its leverage to these markets, as well as growing confidence that the worst is behind the business, have boosted sentiment for these shares.

STMicro shares are up about 20% since my last update, a strong result relative to the broader semiconductor space, as well as more direct comparables like Infineon (IFNNY), NXP Semiconductors (NXPI), ON Semiconductor (ON), and Renesas Electronics (RNECY).

Provided that the ongoing hostilities between the United States, Israel, and Iran don’t trigger a broader downturn for the economy, STMicro’s business should recover in 2026, with notable acceleration in the second half of the year continuing into 2027 and beyond. Moreover, while STMicro does face meaningful competition across multiple markets, opportunities in areas like data centers (or DCs), power, robotics, and sensing do support a more bullish outlook.

A Quick Look Back

Expectations for STMicro’s fourth quarter were modest, and the company basically met them.

Revenue was basically flat from the prior year and up more than 4% from the prior quarter, which was good for a small beat relative to the Street. While the auto business remains pressured (down 15% yoy and up 3% qoq), personal electronics, industrial, and communications markets were all stronger.

Gross margin declined 250bp from the prior year as excess capacity continues to weigh, but did improve about two points sequentially on a better mix. Operating income declined 28% yoy and rose 23% qoq, with margin down about three points yoy to 8%, with pronounced weakness in its auto-driven Power & Discrete Products driving the weakness.

Inventory days improved by 5 to 130 in the fourth quarter, and management expects excess inventory to be worked through by the end of the second quarter. That said, order visibility in the auto market is still problematic, and while industrial demand has improved, it’s coming up from a very low base. More positively, the company continues to gain content with Apple (AAPL) as well as with low-earth orbit satellite manufacturers.

New Content Wins, Recovering Power, And DC Opportunities Driving A Better Revenue Outlook

With the auto sector still accounting for around 40% of demand, this is clearly still an important market for STMicro and arguably the one with the most uncertain outlook today. Overall vehicle production growth is expected to be low in 2026, but semiconductor content continues to grow, particularly in areas like ADAS and controls.

STMicro’s leverage and opportunity here are complicated. While electric vehicle demand should improve and the company shouldn’t see the same share losses with Tesla (TSLA), the company is still highly reliant on Tesla as a customer in power, and rivals like Infineon, NXP, and Renesas have been stepping up their game in MCUs and ADAS products.

While the outlook for STM’s SiC power may be mixed in auto, I’m more confident about the near-term outlook in areas like industrial and DCs, particularly with industrial automation and electrification recovering from sharp recent declines.

New content wins should also help results in 2026. STMicro has won more imaging content on new Apple phones, and there are rumors that the company also won the LiDAR sensor business for the iPhone 18 from Sony (SONY). The company has also been securing wins for other sensor products and its BiCMOS offerings in areas like autos, DCs, and satellites.

Higher Expectations For DC And Physical AI Markets Add To The Story

While a recovery in SiC power and increased iPhone content, as well as underlying recoveries in auto and industrial end-markets, would be enough to drive a stronger second half in 2026, management’s recent commentary on its opportunities in the data center and physical AI (robotics in particular) has boosted confidence in the longer-term secular growth story.

Earlier in March, management hosted a call and presentation on its DC opportunities and ambitions, sizing a $200-$250 million addressable market for every incremental gigawatt of DC capacity. With management expecting over 20% compound annual growth in hyperscaler capex from 2024 to 2030, that works out to over $500 million in 2026 and over $1 billion in 2027.

STMicro is looking to play in both the power and connectivity segments of data centers. For power, management expects the move to 800V to create new opportunities for the company, particularly for SiC and GaN architectures. I believe this is true in principle, but I believe Infineon is better placed at the outset of this opportunity, and STMicro will have to demonstrate that it can transfer capabilities built largely for the auto market to the DC power opportunity.

The connectivity side looks less risky to me, given that the company already has a strong position in areas like BiCMOS electronic ICs (or EICs), microcontrollers, and photonics ICs. With more DCs moving from copper cabling to optical and STMicro ramping up its capabilities and capacity (including a plan to eventually offer co-packaged optics), this could ultimately be the stronger part of this business.

Underlying this opportunity, the company announced a multiyear collaboration with Amazon’s (AMZN) AWS that will see the company provide a wide range of products for AWS DCs, including high-performance mixed-signal processors, advanced microcontrollers, and analog and power ICs. As part of the deal, STMicro is giving Amazon warrants equivalent to almost 25 million shares that will vest over several years—effectively giving Amazon a price discount to secure share/volumes.

Roughly a week later, management hosted another call outlining the company’s leverage in humanoid robotics. Given existing strengths in power, sensing, microcontrollers, and discretes, this is a natural market for STMicro, and I wasn’t all that surprised that the company sized the opportunity at roughly $600 per unit (in line with the $500 quoted by Infineon), or around 20% to 33% of the total COGS of a humanoid robot based on third-party COGS/bill of material estimates.

It’s going to take time for the robotics opportunity to materialize, but STM’s deep experience and strong market positions across power, microcontrollers, and sensing give me more confidence in their ability to secure a good share here in the future. I’d also note that a lot of these components will have broad applicability beyond just humanoid robotics, as many physical AI edge devices and simpler industrial robots will need many of the same components.

The Outlook

For at least the near term, management seems to have successfully redirected the conversation around STMicro from concerns about shaky near-term auto and industrial markets, overreliance on key customers like Apple and Tesla, MCU share losses, and overcapacity to an impending broad-based recovery, share/socket wins, and significant revenue opportunities in DCs and physical AI.

I’d also note that management has been making responsible choices recently regarding capex and capacity. While I do have some concerns that success in the DC and robotics ventures would require more capacity than management currently suggests, capex is under control now, and management just announced that it had started the first deliveries of fully Chinese-produced MCUs for Chinese customers (using Hua Hong as a manufacturing partner).

Between a generally positive reporting cycle for industrial companies, tightening channel inventories, and upgraded DC expectations, my numbers for STMicro are higher now than before, with my 2026 revenue expectation about 6% higher, my 2027 number about 5% higher, and my 2028 number about 4% higher. My long-term revenue growth rate goes from around 6% to 8%, but some of that is also the change in the base year; apples-to-apples, the changes in my modeling assumptions lead to about 50bp higher long-term revenue growth than before.

I haven’t made especially meaningful changes to my margins or cash flow expectations, and I’m still expecting low double-digit operating margins in 2027. Recoveries in areas like SiC power should have a disproportionate positive impact on margins, but I’m also a bit cautious about the pricing of new business wins. Either way, simply seeing higher volumes will have a positive impact on margins in the coming quarters and years.

I’m still expecting low double-digit adjusted free cash flow margins over the long term, and that supports a low double-digit long-term annualized total expected return. Likewise, an operating margin around 12% would still support a 2.45x revenue multiple on FY’27 revenue; discounted back, that gets me to a mid-$30’s fair value today. As was the case before, every incremental point of operating margin is worth about $2/share in additional fair value.

The Bottom Line

In retrospect, it was wrong for me to go “Neutral” on these shares in December on concerns that the Street would be overly concerned about the risks of a recovery more weighted to the second half of 2026. Since then, companies in STMicro’s addressed markets have reported generally positive results and guidance, and management has certainly helped by talking up the opportunities in growth markets like DCs and robotics.

I still see risks here, including the risk of a broad macroeconomic slowdown, further MCU share losses, more intense competition in established auto and industrial markets, and more competition than expected in growth opportunities like DC power and robotics. That said, I also see double-digit return potential, demand coming off a cyclical low, and a management team that has done a better job in recent years than they’ve gotten credit for. With that, I do think these shares are worth considering even after this rebound. I upgrade shares to a "Buy."

data.title: TLT: The Repeating Pattern And The Impending Break
data.description:
I have been holding the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT) since late 2023, and it's been a frustrating ride. Sure, the monthly distributions are nice, but the lack of movement has been frustrating. This article looks at why TLT has remained in a range despite a tumultuous backdrop and what could trigger a break in the pattern and a sustained trend.

A Repetitive Range

TLT has been in a contracting range since it bottomed in 2023. The lack of trend reflects a bond market that is uncertain about the future and unwilling to keep trading in either direction for a sustained period. Any time it seems like one side has the upper hand, the move fades and reverses.

There are many factors driving this behavior, but at its core, two opposing forces are essentially balancing out.

The first major driver comes from growth scares/labor market weakness. This heightens expectations of rate cuts and triggers a TLT rally. That in itself leads to some stabilization as yields drop.

The second opposing force develops as rate cuts are delivered. By this time, the backdrop is already improving, and the outlook steadies. The focus then shifts from the labor market to inflation, and TLT drops again.

As TLT drops, yields rise, growth concerns heighten, and the cycle repeats.

I last wrote about TLT in September 2025, when I warned not to get too excited about rate cuts, as the reaction was likely to be similar to the previous flurry of cuts in late 2024. That largely played out, although the move up and the move down were less volatile.

The reduced volatility may be a function of the market being more prepared and a lack of a surprise factor. The reaction the second time around is rarely as large as the first. Indeed, volatility has trended down for several years and has reached levels last seen in 2018 and 2019 before Covid reversed a trend that lasted for decades.

Of course, there are many other events happening in the background that also have an influence. Geopolitics is a major factor and has triggered two major inflation scares. The first scare in 2025 was triggered by tariffs, and the second by the Iran war and higher oil prices. The interesting thing is they both seem to fit into the pre-existing cycle, as on each occasion the Fed was already finished cutting and had started to shift from labor market concerns to inflation concerns anyway.

A Shifting Fed

During March, the market completely priced out cuts in June and for the rest of 2026. The December rate is currently expected to be unchanged, and a hike is more likely than a cut.

This change was encouraged by the Fed's communication in the March meeting and driven home by a coordinated central bank shift that saw the ECB and BoE lay the groundwork for possible hikes.

Actually, the ECB was already on course for hikes in late 2026, so the oil spike may have just brought this forward. In any case, I don't think central banks have shifted as hawkish as markets seem to think. Higher oil can't be cured by hikes, and any inflation spike will be very different from the wage-price spiral that has only recently been broken. Labor markets have cooled considerably.

I think the Fed will shift back to an easing stance as soon as oil drops or as soon as inflation is considered stable. Alternatively, if the labor market really unravels and we see consistent 4.5% unemployment prints or above, they may look through higher inflation and cut anyway. The new Fed Chair, Kevin Warsh, will surely be pushing for this.

What This Means For TLT

If the current cycle does something similar to the previous cycle, inflation concerns will continue to push TLT lower until yields cause a problem. I'm guessing that won't take too long, as the labor market is still weak and still a concern. Another growth scare could be just around the corner (July/August again?).

The TLT moves are contracting inside the range, but that can't continue indefinitely, so I think we should be prepared for a break in this cycle and behaviour.

What could disrupt the pattern?

It could be another geopolitical event or something exogenous, but I suspect it is simply a case of rate cuts having diminishing effects and the Fed having less room to cut. That means the next flurry of easing may not reverse the next growth scare, and TLT could break the pattern of lower highs.

If stocks drop low enough and rates don't have the desired effect, I suspect Warsh would then push for less traditional easing, i.e., QE. That would be good news for TLT and would—longer-term—provide the usual retracement of the previous bear market.

For context, here is the weekly chart, and it shows that TLT has not even managed to retrace to the 23% Fib of the last decline. A 50-61% retracement is normal.

Near-term, there is a risk of a continued sell-off as the Iran war and higher oil prices weigh, but longer-term, I intend to hold TLT for this larger recovery.

Conclusions

TLT has been stuck in a contracting range for around 30 months now as two opposing forces (labor market and inflation) continue to balance each other out. I expect any further drop in TLT will again lead to another growth scare rally and another flurry of Fed cuts. However, the cycle is weakening, and the contracting range is due to break later this year or in 2027. Should rate cuts not stabilize the next growth scare, TLT likely breaks higher. I remain long and rate TLT a hold or buy on weakness.

Quarterly review

  • The fund outperformed the benchmark S&P 500 Utilities Index for the fourth quarter.
  • Utility holdings slightly underperformed the benchmark.
  • Collectively, the fund's allocation to telecommunications stocks outperformed the index. As a group, the allocation to other sectors also outperformed the benchmark.

Market review

Utilities total return, as measured by the S&P 500 Utilities Index, lagged that of the S&P 500 Index by 4.06% (-1.40% versus 2.66%) during the fourth quarter, finishing the year with underperformance of 1.84% versus the broader index (16.04% versus 17.88%). Investors have generally continued to favor riskier stocks, and the broader market made all-time highs despite meaningful geopolitical and economic concerns. Utilities performed well for most of the year, especially Independent Power Producers (IPPs—companies that produce power on an unregulated basis), driven by consistent and improving fundamentals, attractive valuations, and increased interest in companies that can produce power to supply data center expansion.

Despite a rough start to 2026, we still believe the typically defensive utility sector remains well positioned for whatever macroeconomic scenarios play out in 2026. If the U.S. experiences an economic slowdown, we think regulated utilities' low volatility and steady, predictable earnings and dividend growth would be highly attractive to investors. If, as predicted, U.S. economic growth remains healthy, we feel that many utilities, including the IPPs, are positioned to benefit from rising demand for electricity. Valuation looks attractive as well. The sector recently traded at approximately a 20% discount versus the broader market. We also continue to believe utilities are better positioned for growth than in recent memory. The U.S. looks like it has entered an era of rising electricity demand unseen in decades. Signs of demand growth emerged in 2023 and have since strengthened. Utilities across the country have raised growth forecasts. Beyond the much-discussed proliferation of data centers, utilities have also noted growing electric vehicle penetration and the reshoring of manufacturing to the U.S. as factors driving higher electricity usage. We believe more utilities could raise earnings guidance if demand growth further materializes.

Fund performance and attribution

Contributors

  • Alphabet Inc. (GOOG)
  • Cisco Systems, Inc. (CSCO)
  • Amgen Inc. (AMGN)
  • PPL Corp. (PPL)
  • Duke Energy Corp. (DUK)

Detractors

  • PG&E Corp. (PCG)
  • Edison International (EIX)
  • The Home Depot, Inc. (HD)
  • Meta Platforms, Inc. (META)
  • Comcast Corp. (CMCSA)

The holdings identified do not represent all of the securities purchased or sold during the period shown and should not be construed as a recommendation to purchase or sell a particular security. Information on calculation methodology and a list showing the overall contribution of each holding in the account for the period shown are available upon request.

The fund's utility holdings underperformed the benchmark for the quarter, benefiting from a new position in PPL Corp. (PPL), which has transformed itself into a fully regulated U.S. utility after divesting its U.K. operations. The fund also saw contributions from its underweight positions in Duke Energy Corp. (DUK) and The Southern Company (SO), large-regulated utilities that have not been consistent earnings and dividend growers and do not stand to benefit as much as some small to midsize peers from increasing electricity needs. The primary detractor outweighing these positives was not owning PG&E Corp. (PCG) and Edison International (EIX). These two California utilities have continued to suffer from the lack of a comprehensive solution to the devastating and exceedingly expensive wildfires that have plagued the state in recent years but rallied a bit based on hopes that a constructive solution materializes in 2026.

The largest overall contributor to relative performance for the second straight quarter was the position in Alphabet Inc. Class A (GOOGL), which rallied in our view due to a milder-than-expected antitrust ruling that avoided harsh remedies coupled with accelerating momentum in its artificial intelligence (AI) offerings, particularly in advertising and cloud services. Most other telecommunications-related holdings detracted from performance in the quarter.

The fund invests a portion of the portfolio outside the utilities and telecommunications sectors. These holdings overall outperformed the benchmark in the quarter. Financials holdings Visa Inc. (V) and Mastercard Inc. (MA) outperformed the broader benchmark as the overall market reached new highs. Health care holding Amgen Inc. (AMGN) also outperformed the index as the company reported good quarterly results and increased guidance. The only laggard was The Home Depot, Inc. (HD), which lowered guidance on its quarterly earnings call. We continue to believe that maintaining an allocation to non-utility and telecommunications stocks that offer what we expect to be solid, above-average dividend growth can benefit both overall portfolio dividend growth and total return over time.

Fund positioning and outlook

The One Big Beautiful Bill Act created uncertainty for clean energy spending by rolling back certain tax incentives. While some projects may be delayed or canceled, we believe the ongoing transition to cleaner generation facilities is likely to continue. We think the demand growth many utilities are now forecasting will require an "all of the above" need for new generation capacity: gas, renewables, nuclear, and the delay of coal plant retirements. This should require robust spending on both new generation and expansion of the transmission and distribution grid. Even with a phasing out of tax credits, renewable generation seems well positioned given cost and speed-to-market advantages relative to natural gas and potential new nuclear facilities. The other major trend affecting utilities remains increasing electricity demand. Demand has begun growing and forecasts call for more robust expansion over the next five years. Pursuant to this thesis, IPPs have led the group's stock performance over the past two years. We believe the fund is positioned to benefit from growing demand by owning both IPPs and utilities that own regulated power generation plants.

While capital spending leads to earnings and dividend growth for regulated utilities, rising capital spending is also an important challenge for the sector. Capital investments put pressure on customer bills and affordability concerns continue to mount after several years of above-average inflation. As a result, regulators could choose to limit incremental capital spending to mitigate bill increases. We do not think regulators will meaningfully constrain investment, however, as the majority target the safety and reliability of systems and accommodate the addition of new customers. Furthermore, we think most utilities have done an admirable job limiting expense growth to offset the effect of additional capital spending on customer bills.

Much of the telecommunications sector operates in mature, slow growth markets, including television broadcasting and entertainment, internet access, wireless communications and infrastructure, and data networking. The managers do not expect the environment to change meaningfully in the near term, although in the longer term, emerging use cases in areas like 5G wireless technology, cloud computing, and AI could improve growth prospects. The managers have reduced exposure to these slower growing markets. By adding positions in META and GOOGL, the fund has begun to tilt toward faster-growing communications applications.

Benchmark Descriptions:

The S&P 500 Utilities Index is a market-value-weighted index that measures the performance of all stocks within the utilities sector of the S&P 500 Index. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The S&P 500 Index consists of 500 stocks chosen for market size, liquidity, and industry group representation. It is a market-value-weighted index with each stock's weight in the index proportionate to its market value. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Lipper averages are compiled by Lipper, Inc., an independent mutual fund research and rating service. Each Lipper average represents a universe of funds that are similar in investment objective. You cannot invest directly in a Lipper average.

The Morningstar Category average is the average return for the peer group based on the returns of each individual fund within the group. The total return of the Morningstar Category average does not include the effect of sales charges. You cannot invest directly in a Morningstar Category average.

Attribution Analysis:

Performance attribution and sector returns are calculated using the Brinson-Fachler attribution model. As such, performance attribution calculations may differ from the fund's actual investment results. Common sources of attribution dispersion from official returns include: the use of end of day holdings and prices, the exclusion of fees, and the use of myriad pricing sources to accommodate different benchmark providers.

Definition Of Terms:

Alpha measures the difference between a fund's actual returns and its expected performance given its level of risk (as measured by beta).

Beta measures fund volatility relative to general market movements. It is a standardized measure of systematic risk in comparison with a specified index. The benchmark beta is 1.00 by definition.

Downside capture measures a fund's replication of its benchmark during periods of negative returns. During periods of negative benchmark returns, a downside capture ratio less than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and a downside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Information ratio measures the consistency of excess return (return in excess of a benchmark). This value is determined by taking the annualized excess return over a benchmark (style benchmark by default) and dividing it by the standard deviation of excess return.

R-squared is a measurement of how similar a fund's historical performance has been to that of the benchmark. The measure ranges from 0.0, which means that the fund's performance bears no relationship to the performance of the index, to 1.0, which means that the fund's performance was perfectly synchronized with the performance of the benchmark.

Sharpe ratio measures the potential reward offered by a mutual fund relative to its risk level. The ratio uses a fund's standard deviation and its excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. The higher the Sharpe ratio, the better the fund's historical risk-adjusted performance.

Standard deviation represents the degree to which an investment's performance has varied from its average performance over a particular time period.

Tracking error measures the extent to which a manager's performance mimics that of a benchmark. The value is the standard deviation of the difference between a fund's performance and a benchmark's performance.

Upside capture measures a fund's replication of its benchmark during periods of positive returns. During periods of positive benchmark returns, an upside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and an upside capture ratio less than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.
