data.title: Dow Jones And U.S. Stock Market Outlook - Wall Street Uncertain Amid U.S.-Iran (Potential) Talks
data.description:
By Elior Manier

US equity benchmarks staged a solid rebound yesterday, but once again failed to hold onto their highs — a pattern that is starting to feel all too familiar in this environment.

Despite some easing in broader sentiment, helped by oil trading well below its Globex open, the overall tone remains fragile. Markets are struggling to find clear direction as the fundamental backdrop stays clouded, leaving indices mixed into today’s session.

Under the surface, weakness is beginning to show. This marks a second consecutive day of broad participation, but some of the largest names are weakening. Microsoft (MSFT) and Alphabet (GOOGL) are both down around 2%, dragging on the Nasdaq and maintaining the pressure on tech.

The broader issue lies in geopolitics. While US-Iran talks may have quietly started, markets remain highly skeptical about the feasibility of any meaningful breakthrough. That caution has only been reinforced by Saudi Arabia shifting toward a more aggressive stance, adding another layer of uncertainty to an already fragile narrative.

As a result, markets appear stuck at a pressure point — reacting less to headlines, yet unable to fully push higher. It’s a classic sign of hesitation, where positioning is light, conviction is low, and participants are waiting for clearer signals before committing.

This morning’s PMI releases did little to help, coming in mixed with manufacturing posing a large surprise but services slowing down.

Private credit is also making the headlines once again, with Apollo Global Management, a large asset manager, restricting withdrawals to 5% amid continued outflows in the space – This remains one of the more concerning financial catalysts in markets, which deserves some attention.

Financials are remaining unscathed for now, but don't forget to monitor this situation.

Let's spot where today's rough price action is heading by looking at today’s intraday charts and trading levels for the major US indexes: the Dow Jones, Nasdaq, and S&P 500.

Current Session's Stock Heatmap

Today's sector action is broadly positive, supported by the beat in manufacturing PMIs and pulling the Dow on top, while other indexes struggle.

Producer Manufacturing and Utilities are the large outperformers for yet another session, while Tech struggles to find meaningful balance.

Dow Jones 1H Chart and Trading Levels

Dow Jones (CFD) 1H Chart – March 24, 2026 – Source: TradingView

The DJIA is forming a slightly bullish price action but is still struggling to break above its 200-hour moving average – clearing it leaves space for bulls.

On the other hand, rejecting it would maintain the broader downward channel.

Nasdaq 1H Chart and Trading Levels

Nasdaq (CFD) 1H Chart – March 24, 2026 – Source: TradingView

Nasdaq is stuck in a bizarre consolidation, having rejected its previous session's spike and now testing the lower regions of its range.

The 24,150 to 24,200 support, testing immediately, will be acting as a major guide to the action – With a triangle formation's upper and lower boundaries defined by the support zone, look for either a break above or below to play breakouts.

The immediate outlook is more balanced than anything as traders await for headlines.

Nasdaq technical levels of interest:

data.title: Silver: Explosive Upside Potential Amidst Iran War
data.description:
A Key Catalyst Has Now Materialized

As we approach the end of March 2026, I thought it was an opportune time to update my investment thesis on silver (XAGUSD:CUR). As illustrated below, my last article on the subject was published in early February 2026, right after one of the most violent market crashes in silver's history. Almost two months later, one of the most important catalysts underpinning my bullish stance on precious metals has now materialized, and this is what I would like to discuss in more detail today. The quote below is an excerpt from my previous article published in early January 2026.

In my personal view, the next crisis is likely to be triggered by a major geopolitical event. The Federal Reserve would have to intervene once again to liquify the bond markets, and fiscal deficits would surge to new record highs in line with military expenditures.

As a quick reminder, silver futures traded on the COMEX went from a high of $121.79 on January 29 to a low of $74.00 on January 30, the equivalent of a ~40% decline in less than 24 hours. As I am writing this article, silver is hovering around $67.00 an ounce, after reaching a low of $61.21 earlier in the week. In other words, we are now in the midst of a ~50% drawdown, and I believe it creates a wonderful opportunity to increase my long position once again.

I began accumulating silver back in December 2023 when it was still trading in the low $20s, and over the years, it has grown into the second-largest position in my investment portfolio. I would also say that among the 142 articles I have published so far since joining Seeking Alpha in September 2024, my thesis on silver is the most important of all, hence why I thought it would be appropriate to give an update today.

Iran Closing The Strait Of Hormuz

I discussed my concerns about the Strait of Hormuz for the first time on this platform back in November 2024, as part of an article on Danaos Corporation (DAC), one of the world's largest container ship owners. In other publications, I have also talked about the Bank of England's system-wide exploratory scenario exercise in which a 'sudden crystallization of geopolitical tensions' leads to a shock to global financial markets and triggers a rapid rise on sovereign bond yields across the Western world. Now that the Strait of Hormuz has been closed for almost a month, and that some of the most important energy infrastructure in the region has also been attacked, it seems like geopolitical risks that have been lurking around for years are materializing in real time. Even though silver prices have pulled back so far, I think those material geopolitical developments will push silver prices much higher over the long term.

The deteriorating conditions in the Middle East are slowly putting the Strait of Hormuz into focus. Even though this route is absolutely vital for oil trade, it is less so for containership. In my view, the real deal when it comes to geopolitical risk is the Taiwan Strait, and if Hormuz is the world's jugular vein for oil, the Taiwan Strait and the South China Sea are the jugulars for containerships.

As illustrated below, commercial vessel crossings through the Hormuz Strait have collapsed to almost zero starting in early March, and this has obviously put significant upside pressure on oil prices around the globe. Knowing that the Strait accounts for ~25% of the world's seaborne oil trade, this is pretty much equivalent to clogging the jugular vein of the world economy. In addition, we have also reached the point that even if Hormuz opens back up tomorrow morning, it would not bring back the lost production capacity associated with the now damaged energy infrastructure.

If we see silver in particular and precious metals in general as units of stored energy, it implies that a world characterized by higher energy prices should act as a tailwind for higher metal prices. For those familiar with cryptocurrencies, this is the equivalent of saying that a higher hash rate or mining difficulty should be associated with higher proof-of-work prices, meaning higher Bitcoin (BTC-USD) or Litecoin (LTC-USD) prices, for example.

Surging diesel prices are also a reminder of the risk involved with mining companies, as the benefits associated with higher metal prices will be partially offset by higher operating costs. Exchange-traded products like the iShares Silver Trust (SLV), the Sprott Physical Silver Trust (PSLV) or the abrdn Physical Silver Shares (SIVR) are obviously immune to this risk, as is also the case with royalty companies like Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM) or by owning the physical metal outright. I also think that geopolitical and jurisdictional risks are more important than ever before for anyone exposed to silver miners. And as usual, geological and metallurgical risks remain part of the game also. Those are some of the key reasons why I prefer to avoid the idiosyncratic risks associated with miners, and to remain focused on a pure-play exposure through the physical metal itself, ETFs or royalty companies.

Fiscal Deficits About To Surge

Just last week, the U.S. national debt surpassed $39 trillion for the first time, which represents an increase of $1 trillion in five short months. If we go back to the days just prior to the COVID-19 pandemic, the U.S. national debt stood at around $23.5 billion, meaning that it increased by almost 70% over the last six years.

With surging energy prices threatening to unleash another wave of inflation on the world economy, we should not be surprised to see long-term Treasury yields edge higher also. Given the amount of debt outstanding today, sovereign bond markets have never been so vulnerable to rising yields, especially knowing that the U.S. Treasury paid $1.0 trillion of interest on the debt in fiscal 2025, which surpassed defense spending for the second year in a row.

In other words, the ongoing conflict in the Middle East is pushing energy prices higher, which in turn leads to higher bond yields and fiscal deficits. This is precisely when silver's monetary function as a hard asset comes into play, and of course, this is also true for gold (XAUUSD:CUR). With a rapidly growing amount of paper claims chasing a finite amount of tangible assets, I believe we are still early in this precious metals bull market.

In my view, what is going on with Iran and the Strait of Hormuz today is a major catalyst that will ultimately lead to even more money printing. Just a few days ago, the Pentagon was reportedly looking for $200 billion to fund the ongoing operations in the Middle East. In a way, the conflict with Iran is a double blow for the fiscal deficits of the United States, as interest and military expenses are both about to surge higher. In this context, where is the safe haven? I don't think there is one single answer to this question, but I do know that a precious metal like silver is clearly a part of the solution.

Industrial And Military Demand For Silver

Now that I have discussed why I believe the monetary demand for silver is about to surge higher, I would like to touch upon the second component of silver demand, which is its mission-critical role within a wide range of industrial and military applications. According to a February 2026 press release from the Silver Institute, 2026 is expected to mark the sixth consecutive year of deficit for silver, with rising demand showing no sign of stopping.

In my view, one of the best ways to visualize silver's supply and demand imbalance is to look at the amount of metal sitting in the COMEX's vaults over time. As illustrated below, the total amount of registered silver declined from about 200 million ounces in the fall of 2025 to less than 80 million ounces at the moment, the equivalent of a ~60% decline in a few short months. The inventory of registered silver is becoming increasingly relevant as it represents metals that can be readily used to settle the paper contracts into actual metal.

I don't think it is a coincidence that the U.S. Geological Survey added silver to its list of critical minerals back in November 2025. Given the rising geopolitical tensions all around the world, it is also worth mentioning that defence contractors like Lockheed Martin (LMT), RTX Corporation (RTX), Northrop Grumman (NOC) or General Dynamics (GD) are among the largest industrial users of silver. As discussed in the research paper 'Strategic Utilization and Shielding Capabilities of Silver in U.S. Defence Applications' published in April 2024, silver fulfills a multifaceted role in today's defence industry:

The U.S. Department of Defense (DoD) leverages silver extensively, though often under a veil of discretion due to the strategic nature of its applications. Silver's superior electrical properties make it indispensable in high-end military communications equipment, aerospace technology, and other defense electronics, where reliability and performance are paramount. This strategic deployment underscores the need for a robust understanding and management of silver resources, especially given its significant import reliance.

If we go back a decade in time, silver demand was initially propelled by the growing usage of photovoltaics. This trend is still ongoing today, except that another layer of demand was added on top of it over the last few years, which is the rising popularity of electric vehicles. Looking into the future, more and more Chinese auto manufacturers are about to start testing solid-state batteries in their vehicles. This new advancement in battery technology allows for faster charging and autonomy, although it also comes with a much greater silver intensity. For example, Samsung's new solid-state battery with a silver-carbon composite layer for the anode is estimated to contain 5 grams of silver in every battery cell, which translates into 1 kilogram of silver for a 100 kWh battery pack.

On top of that, the ongoing and historical AI infrastructure boom is also consuming an increasing amount of silver, as it is also a critical element within NVIDIA's (NVDA) GPUs. And now, with rising geopolitical tensions in the Middle East, I would expect any meaningful surge in defense spending to ultimately push silver demand even higher. As a reminder, the total supply of silver has remained pretty much flat over the last decade or so, going from 1.06 billion ounces in 2016 to 1.03 billion ounces in 2025. Using the Silver Institute's most recent projections released in February 2026, total supply is forecasted at 1.05 billion in fiscal 2026. Even if it is a slight improvement from previous years, it is far from enough to reestablish balance between supply and demand.

Knowing that the demand for silver is in structural and long-term growth, where will the physical metal come from? Creating paper silver on the COMEX is one thing, but unfortunately, it is quite useless when it comes to manufacturing solar panels, solid-state batteries, AI datacenters or Tomahawk missiles.

Considering that silver is primarily mined as a by-product of gold and base metals mines, I would not expect primary mine production to increase meaningfully from here. If we also account for geological and regulatory constraints, I think forecasting for stable mine production over the next few years is quite optimistic in itself. On the other hand, I would not be surprised to see silver recycling increase along with higher metal prices. On a standalone basis, this is not a supply source that can solve the ongoing supply and demand imbalance, even though it is probably the brightest spot in silver's overall supply picture. In this context, the only remaining lever to meet the growing demand shortfall is through above-ground stockpiles. And for that, silver prices will have to increase high enough to trigger a commensurate response from those holding the physical metal itself.

Conclusion

In summary, the start of the Iran war and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz are acting as important catalysts for my overall investment thesis on silver, starting with rising energy and diesel prices that are likely to push silver's production costs higher. In addition, higher energy prices are also putting upward pressure on long-term yields, which then leads to higher interest expenses and ultimately to bigger fiscal deficits for highly indebted countries like the United States and most Western countries.

With a fiscal deficit expected between $1.7 and $1.9 trillion, the only way for the U.S. Government to balance its budget is to borrow. Given the historical events that have happened in the Middle East over the last few weeks, this borrowing spree could soon lead to another round of money printing, and considering that the hole to plug gets larger and larger after every crisis, I would expect the next printing round to be even larger than what we all experienced during the COVID-19 pandemic. From my perspective, all of those elements could lead to accelerating investment demand for silver.

On the industrial side, the picture is pretty much the same, as higher military spending only adds another layer of demand for the physical metal. Solid-state batteries are also starting to gain traction in the Chinese EV market, and the investment case of photovoltaics is only getting stronger with surging hydrocarbon prices.

When I look at the overall picture, I see all of those elements converging towards one conclusion, which is higher demand. The problem is that the silver market is now in its sixth year of deficits while the metal's supply function is largely inelastic.

The reiteration of my 'Strong Buy' rating reflects my personal view that now is truly a unique opportunity to acquire silver on the cheap. Despite the materialization of a significant catalyst, silver prices are down by about 50% from the all-time high reached at the end of January 2026. I believe it is only a matter of time before we reach new highs, while also keeping in mind that the second leg of this bull market could be much bigger than the first one.
